cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are going deep into a book that has quietly rearranged the way a lot of successful people work and live.
It is called The ONE Thing: The Surprisingly Simple Truth Behind Extraordinary Results, written by Gary Keller with Jay Papasan.
Gary Keller built one of the largest real estate companies in the world, and somewhere along that climb he realized something almost embarrassing in its simplicity.
Whenever things went right in his business and in his life, it was because he had narrowed his focus down to what mattered most.
And whenever things fell apart, it was because he had let his focus scatter across too many things at once.
That is the whole book in one breath.
But do not let the simplicity fool you, because simple and easy are not the same thing.
What Keller is asking you to do runs against almost every instinct you have been trained to follow.
So settle in, because for the next stretch of time, I want to help you rewire the way you think about getting things done, and about what a meaningful life actually requires.
Let me start with a picture.
Imagine a single domino standing on a table.
Nothing special about it.
Now imagine you could line up dominoes, but each one you set down is slightly bigger than the one before it.
Here is the part that most people never learn.
A single domino can knock over another domino that is about fifty percent larger than itself.
Sit with that for a second.
A little domino, with just the energy of a tap and gravity, can topple something half again its size.
And that larger domino, when it falls, can topple one that is fifty percent larger still.
So the growth is not a straight line where you add a little each time.
The growth is geometric.
It multiplies.
A physicist named Lorne Whitehead wrote about this back in 1983, and the numbers are genuinely hard to believe.
If you start with a domino just two inches tall and you keep making each one fifty percent bigger, the eighteenth domino in that chain would be as tall as the Leaning Tower of Pisa.
The twenty-third domino would tower over the Eiffel Tower.
The thirty-first domino would stand taller than Mount Everest.
And by the fifty-seventh domino, that final domino would nearly reach the moon.
From a two-inch starting block, with nothing but a small push, you get a chain that could knock down something as tall as the distance from the Earth to the moon.
Now here is why that matters to your life.
Extraordinary results are not about doing a thousand things.
They are about lining up the dominoes.
It is about finding the right first thing, the small thing you can actually do today, and knocking it over, so it sets up the next thing, which sets up the next thing.
Success builds on success.
When you look at people who have accomplished something remarkable, it can look like they toppled a giant domino out of nowhere.
But if you trace it backward, you almost always find a lead domino, a first move, that made the second move possible, that made the third possible, and on and on.
Their success was sequential.
It was built one falling piece at a time.
So when you feel overwhelmed, when you look at the giant thing you want and it feels impossibly far away, remember the dominoes.
You do not have to knock over the mountain today.
You have to find the small domino in front of you, the one that starts the chain, and you have to knock that one over.
This brings us to the beating heart of the entire book.
Keller calls it the Focusing Question, and I want you to actually memorize it, because it is a tool you will use for the rest of your life.
Here it is.
What is the ONE Thing I can do such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary?
Read it again in your mind.
What is the ONE Thing I can do such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary?
Every word in that question is load-bearing, so let me pull it apart for you.
The ONE Thing tells you to go small, to force a single answer instead of a list.
I can do makes it about action, something you actually control, not a wish.
Such that is a powerful little phrase, because it demands a connection, a cause and effect.
And here is the magic ending.
By doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary.
That is the leverage test.
You are not just looking for something important.
You are looking for the domino that makes the other dominoes fall on their own.
You are looking for the action that either shrinks your whole to-do list or wipes parts of it out entirely.
Most people, when they wake up, ask themselves what they need to get done today, and they generate a giant list, and they treat every item on it as roughly equal.
Keller wants you to ask a sharper question.
Not what can I do, but what is the ONE thing that makes everything else easier or unnecessary.
Because when you find that thing and you do it first, the rest of the day falls into place around it.
You can apply the Focusing Question to anything.
Your work today.
Your career this year.
Your health.
Your relationships.
Your finances.
Your spiritual life.
You just narrow the frame.
What is the ONE Thing I can do this week for my marriage such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary?
What is the ONE Thing I can do to hit my sales goal such that everything else becomes easier or unnecessary?
The question scales up and down, from your whole life down to this very moment.
Now, before we go further, we have to clear away the garbage.
Because you have been fed a set of ideas your whole life that quietly sabotage your ability to focus.
Keller calls them the six lies between you and success.
They sound like wisdom.
Some of them are printed on motivational posters.
But they will scatter you, and I want you to see through each one.
The first lie is that everything matters equally.
This is the tyranny of the to-do list.
You write down twenty things and you tell yourself they all need doing, so you attack them in whatever order they occur to you, usually starting with the easiest or the loudest.
But a to-do list is just a list of everything you think you should do.
It is not a plan.
It is not a strategy.
The truth is that things are never equal.
In any list of tasks, a few of them matter enormously and most of them barely matter at all.
Achievers do not treat their list as a democracy.
They turn their to-do list into a success list.
They ask, out of all of this, what is the most important thing, the thing that if I do it makes the rest easier or irrelevant, and then they do that first.
A to-do list tends to be long.
A success list is short.
One pulls you in every direction.
The other points you in a single direction.
Do not let a long list of good things crowd out the one great thing.
The second lie is that multitasking works.
This one is worth a real conversation, because our whole culture worships at the altar of multitasking.
We brag about it in job interviews.
We answer email during meetings, we text while we talk, we think we are being efficient.
But the research is brutal and clear.
You cannot actually do two things that require attention at the same time.
What you are really doing is task switching, flipping your attention back and forth, and every switch has a cost.
Your brain has to stop, disengage from one thing, reorient to the other, and reload the context.
Do that over and over and you bleed time and quality out of everything you touch.
The numbers on this are alarming.
Researchers have estimated that this kind of switching can eat up a huge portion of your productive time, and worse, it makes you more error-prone.
There is a famous line of research where people were asked to focus while distractions were introduced, and their performance fell off a cliff.
Think about the two-things-at-once trap in a car.
You know, deep down, that texting while driving is dangerous, and the reason is not that your hands are busy.
It is that your attention is somewhere else.
Your brain went to the phone, and for those seconds the road did not exist for you.
Multitasking is not doing more.
It is doing more poorly.
It is the opposite of the focus that produces extraordinary results.
When you try to do two things at once, you either cannot or will not do either well.
So stop wearing multitasking as a badge.
It is not a superpower.
It is a tax.
The third lie is that a disciplined life is the answer.
We look at high achievers and we assume they are just superhumanly disciplined people, that they gut through everything by sheer force of will, that discipline saturates every corner of their lives.
That picture is wrong, and believing it will exhaust you.
Here is the truth that Keller offers, and it is genuinely freeing.
You do not need to be a disciplined person.
You need, in fact, just enough discipline to build one powerful habit at a time.
Success is about doing the right thing, not about doing everything right.
The people who look disciplined are not fighting themselves all day long.
They have built habits, and habits run on autopilot.
The discipline was spent up front, in the weeks it took to make the behavior automatic.
Once the habit is set, it carries them without the constant drain of willpower.
And how long does it take to build a habit?
The book points to research suggesting the average is about sixty-six days.
Not twenty-one days, which is a myth that has floated around for decades.
Around sixty-six days of consistent effort for a new behavior to become automatic, give or take depending on the person and the difficulty.
So the play is not to become a maximally disciplined person overnight across every area of your life.
The play is to pick one habit, the right one, and pour your discipline into it for a couple of months until it locks in and runs itself.
Then, and only then, you layer on the next habit.
Selected discipline, applied to one thing at a time in sequence, builds a life that looks effortlessly disciplined from the outside.
Trying to be disciplined about everything at once builds nothing but burnout.
The fourth lie is closely tied to the third.
It is the belief that willpower is always on will and able.
We treat willpower as if it is a character trait, something you either have or lack, and something that is available to you whenever you decide to summon it.
But that is not how willpower works.
Willpower is more like a muscle that tires, or better yet, like the battery on your phone.
In the morning it is fully charged.
But every act of self-control, every decision you make, every temptation you resist, every distraction you push away, draws down that battery.
By late afternoon or evening, you are running on a low charge, and that is exactly when your resolve caves and you make the choices you later regret.
There is a wonderful and slightly cruel experiment that illustrates this perfectly.
Researchers brought people into a room that smelled of freshly baked chocolate chip cookies.
On the table sat a bowl of those warm cookies and a bowl of radishes.
One group was told they could eat the cookies.
The other group, the unlucky ones, were told they could only eat the radishes, and they had to resist the cookies sitting right there in front of them.
Then both groups were given a difficult, frustrating geometry puzzle to solve.
The puzzle was actually impossible, but they did not know that.
The researchers were measuring how long people would persist before giving up.
The cookie eaters, who had spent no willpower resisting anything, worked on the puzzle for about nineteen minutes on average.
The radish eaters, who had just burned through a big chunk of their willpower forcing themselves not to eat the cookies, gave up after only about eight minutes.
They had less than half the persistence.
Why?
Because self-control is a finite resource, and they had spent theirs on the cookies.
This is what psychologists call decision fatigue, or ego depletion.
The more choices and self-restraint you demand of yourself, the weaker your willpower gets as the day goes on.
Here is the life-changing takeaway from that.
If willpower is a limited, rechargeable resource, then you should not leave your most important work to whenever you happen to get to it.
You should do your ONE Thing early, when your willpower battery is full.

Do not spend your peak willpower hours on email and small decisions and other people's priorities, and then try to do your most important, most demanding work at four in the afternoon when you have nothing left in the tank.
Feed your willpower first, protect it, and spend it on what matters most, before the world drains it away.
Timing is not a detail.
Timing is strategy.
The fifth lie is the seductive idea of a balanced life.
This might be the most sacred cow of them all.
We are told constantly to seek balance, to give equal attention to work, family, health, friends, hobbies, everything, all in careful proportion.
It sounds so healthy and so wise.
And Keller says, gently but firmly, that it is a lie.
A perfectly balanced life is a myth, and chasing it is a recipe for mediocrity, because the middle is where nothing extraordinary ever happens.
Think about it.
Extraordinary results, in any domain, require concentrated blocks of time and attention.
And any time you pour yourself deeply into one thing, something else, by definition, gets less of you in that moment.
That is not a failure of balance.
That is the price of doing anything meaningful.
So what do you do instead?
You do not balance.
You counterbalance.
Keller draws a distinction between two kinds of counterbalancing.
In your professional life, the counterbalancing needs to be long.
You go extreme.
You allow yourself to lean hard into your ONE Thing, to be deliberately out of balance in favor of your priority for extended stretches, because that is what mastery and big results demand.
You short-change the other work things on purpose, knowing you will come back to them.
In your personal life, the counterbalancing needs to be short.
You do not want to be extremely out of balance at home for long, because the people you love and your own health cannot be put on hold for months.
So in your personal life, you never let any single area go too far out of whack for too long.
You catch it quickly.
You lean back the other way before real damage is done.
The word to hold onto is counterbalance, not balance.
You are never standing perfectly still in the center.
You are always leaning, adjusting, correcting, going long in your work priority and staying nimble in your personal life.
A worthwhile life is not one where every plate spins at the same steady height.
It is one where you know which plate matters most right now and you give it what it needs, and you trust yourself to swing back to the others before they crash.
Give up on the fantasy of perfect balance.
Trade it for the reality of purposeful counterbalancing.
The sixth and final lie is that big is bad.

Somewhere along the way, a lot of us picked up the belief that thinking big is dangerous, that big goals lead to big stress, big risk, big failure, that we should be realistic and keep our ambitions modest and safe.
Keller flips this completely.
He argues that big is not bad.
Big is often exactly what you should aim for, because the size of your thinking shapes the size of your results and, just as importantly, the size of the person you have to become to achieve them.
When you set a big goal, you are forced to grow, to learn, to build new skills and new habits, to line up bigger dominoes.
If you only ever chase small goals, you never stretch, and you never discover what you are capable of.
He makes a fascinating point about fear here.
What we call being realistic is very often just fear wearing a respectable disguise.
People play small because playing big is scary, and then they tell themselves and everyone else that they are just being sensible.
But avoiding a big life to avoid the discomfort of growth is a bad trade.
The people who achieve extraordinary things are not necessarily braver or smarter.
They just allowed themselves to think bigger, and then they let that big vision pull them forward.
So do not fear big.
Fear small.
Fear a life so cautious that you never find out how far your dominoes could have gone.
Now that we have knocked down those six lies, let me put the two central skills back on the table clearly, because the whole book really turns on them.
The first is going small.
The second is thinking big.
And they are not a contradiction.
They are a partnership.
You think big to know where you are going, to set the direction, to pick the mountain.
And then you go small to know what to do right now, to find the lead domino, the single next action.
Think big, act small.
Your vision can be enormous, but your focus in any given moment must be laser-narrow.
Big vision, small focus.
That is the rhythm of extraordinary results.
To help you go small, Keller leans on a principle you have probably heard of, and then he pushes it further than most people ever take it.
It is the Pareto principle, the eighty-twenty rule.
An Italian economist named Vilfredo Pareto noticed that about eighty percent of the effects come from about twenty percent of the causes.
Eighty percent of the wealth was held by twenty percent of the people.
And over time we have seen this pattern show up everywhere.
A minority of your customers drive the majority of your revenue.
A minority of your efforts produce the majority of your results.
A minority of the items on your list matter more than all the rest combined.
So the first move is to find the twenty percent that matters and pour yourself into it.
But here is where Keller gets bold.
He says do not stop at the twenty percent.
Take that critical twenty percent and apply the same thinking again.
Out of that vital twenty percent, what is the top twenty percent of that?
And then do it again.
Keep narrowing, keep cutting, keep asking which few things inside the few things matter most, until you cannot cut any further, until you are left standing in front of a single item.
That last item, the thing you cannot reduce any further, that is your ONE Thing.
Most people stop at a short list and feel good about it.
Keller wants you to keep going until the list becomes one.
The extraordinary achiever is ruthless about this.
They are not looking for a handful of priorities.
The very word priority, for most of its history, existed only in the singular.
There was one priority, the first thing, the prior thing.
Only in modern times did we start talking about priorities, plural, as if we could have many first things.
You cannot.
You can only have one true priority at a time.
Find it.
Okay.
So let us say you have done the work.
You have thought big, you have narrowed down, you have found your ONE Thing.
How do you actually make sure it gets done in a world that is constantly grabbing at your time?
The answer is one of the most practical and important ideas in the whole book, and it is called time blocking.
Time blocking is exactly what it sounds like.
You take your calendar and you carve out a specific, protected block of time dedicated to your ONE Thing, and you treat it as the most sacred appointment on your schedule.
Not something you will get to if there is time left over.
The most important thing, placed first, and defended fiercely.
Keller suggests blocking a serious chunk of time, ideally in the morning when your willpower battery is full, four hours if you can manage it, for your most important work.
And when I say defend it fiercely, I mean it.
During your ONE Thing time block, the door is closed, the phone is off, the notifications are silenced, and the answer to interruptions is no.
Keller tells a story about protecting time that has stuck with me.
He talks about the mindset you need, which is to guard your time block the way a mother bear guards her cubs.
Ferociously.
Because the world will not respect your time block for you.
Colleagues will want to meet.
Emails will pile up.
Someone will always have an urgent request that they would love to make your problem.
Every one of those is a hand reaching in to steal your ONE Thing time.
And you have to be the person who says, this time is already spoken for, and means it.
There is a wonderful spirit in the book of treating the question who stole my time as a mystery you refuse to keep being the victim of.
The thief is almost always us, giving our time away in small yeses to other people's priorities.
Time blocking is how you stop the theft before it happens.
And it is not just your ONE Thing that deserves a block.
Keller says you should also block time for planning.
Set aside time, perhaps an hour each week, to look at where you are relative to your bigger goals and to decide what your ONE Thing should be for the coming period.
Planning time is how you make sure the dominoes are lined up correctly before you start knocking them over.
So three kinds of blocks.
Block time for your ONE Thing, and make it the biggest and earliest.
Block time for planning.
And block a little time off, for rest and renewal, because you cannot pour from an empty cup and your willpower battery needs to recharge.
But the non-negotiable, the one that changes everything, is the ONE Thing block.
Put it on the calendar first, before anything else claims the space, and let the rest of your day arrange itself around it.
Now, protecting that block is a battle, and to help you win it, Keller identifies what he calls the four thieves of productivity.
These are the forces that come to rob you of your focused time, and if you can name them, you can fight them.
The first thief is the inability to say no.
Every time you say yes to something, you are saying no to something else, whether you realize it or not.
And usually the thing you are unknowingly saying no to is your ONE Thing.
People who protect their focus have to become comfortable, even generous, in the way they say no.
Keller points out that saying no is not rude.
It is often the kindest and most honest thing you can do, because a distracted yes helps no one.
You can say no graciously, you can say not right now, you can point someone to a better resource, but you have to be willing to say it.
Every no you say to a distraction is a yes to your priority.
Protect the one by declining the many.
The second thief is the fear of chaos.
When you commit to your ONE Thing and let everything else wait, other things will pile up.
Your inbox will overflow.
Some tasks will slip.
And that messiness creates anxiety, a nagging feeling that you are letting things fall apart, that you should stop and clean it all up.
Keller wants you to make peace with that mess.
Pursuing your ONE Thing will always create some collateral chaos, and that is not a sign you are doing it wrong.
It is a sign you are doing it right.
You cannot achieve something extraordinary and keep everything else in perfect order at the same time.
Accept the chaos as the cost of focus, and trust that you will clean it up later.
Do not let a messy desk pull you off the one thing that actually moves your life forward.
The third thief is poor health habits.
This one surprises people, but it is deeply true.
Your ability to focus, your willpower, your energy, all of it runs on your body.
If you are not sleeping enough, not eating well, not moving your body, not managing stress, then your battery starts each day at half charge, and it drains faster.
High achievement is not sustained by burning yourself out.
It is sustained by taking care of the machine.

Keller even sketches out a sort of ideal morning that feeds your energy before you spend it, time to relax and reflect, to eat well, to exercise, to connect with the people you love, so that you arrive at your ONE Thing time block fully powered.
Guard your health, because your health is the power source for everything else.
The fourth thief is an environment that does not support your goals.
Your environment is made up of two things.
The people around you and the physical space you live and work in.
Both of them are constantly shaping you, for better or worse.
If the people around you are negative, distracting, or pulling you away from your priorities, they will drag you down no matter how strong your intentions are.
And if your physical environment is full of temptations and interruptions, you will lose the battle for focus before it even starts.
So the move is to deliberately build an environment that supports your ONE Thing.
Surround yourself with the right people, people who encourage your focus and model the success you want.
And engineer your physical space to make focus easy and distraction hard.
Do not rely on willpower to overcome a bad environment.
Change the environment so you do not have to.
Those are the four thieves.
The inability to say no.
The fear of chaos.
Poor health habits.
And an unsupportive environment.
Name them when they come for your time, and you will be far more likely to hold your ground.
Alongside the four thieves, Keller lays out three commitments you have to make if you want to reach the highest levels of achievement.
These are internal shifts, changes in how you carry yourself toward your work.
The first commitment is to follow the path of mastery.
Mastery is not a destination you arrive at.
It is a path you commit to walking, probably for the rest of your life.
When you commit to mastering your ONE Thing, you approach it like an apprentice, always learning, always practicing, always pushing to be better than you were.
Most people plateau because they get comfortable at good enough.
The masters never stop.
They treat their craft as something they are always deepening.
So commit to the path.
Decide that your ONE Thing is something you will keep getting better at forever, not something you will do until it is fine.
The second commitment is to move from what Keller calls the entrepreneurial approach to the purposeful approach.
When we start something new, we naturally do it the entrepreneurial way, which just means we do it with our natural energy and instinct, throwing ourselves at it however we know how.
That works, up to a point.
But eventually you hit a ceiling, the limit of what your natural ability alone can achieve.
At that ceiling, most people either give up or settle.
The high achiever does something different.
They become purposeful.
They seek out the best models, the best systems, the proven methods, and they adopt them even when it means changing how they have always done things.
Moving from entrepreneurial to purposeful, from E to P, means being willing to trade your comfortable natural approach for a better one that breaks through the ceiling.
It is the willingness to say, my usual way has taken me as far as it can, so I will go learn the way that works even better.
The third commitment is to live the accountability cycle rather than the victim cycle.
This might be the most important of the three.
When things go wrong, and they will, you have a choice about how you interpret it.
The victim looks outward for blame.
It was the market, the boss, the circumstances, bad luck, other people.
The accountable person looks at the same situation and asks, given this reality, what can I do about it?
Accountable people take ownership of their results, good and bad.
They do not waste energy on excuses.
They spend it on solutions.
Keller is clear that accountability is one of the most powerful forces in high achievement, and it is a choice you make over and over.
So when the setback comes, catch yourself in the moment of blame, and turn it around.
Ask what is mine to own here, and what will I do next.
That single shift, from victim to accountable, changes the entire trajectory of a life.
Now I want to bring in one more tool, and this one ties the whole book together, because it connects your grandest dreams to the tiny action in front of you right now.
Keller calls it Goal Setting to the Now.
It is the bridge between the big and the small, between vision and today.
Here is how it works.
You start with your someday goal.
What is the ONE Thing you want to accomplish someday, the big life goal, the mountain?
Hold that in your mind.
Now ask, based on my someday goal, what is the ONE Thing I can do in the next five years to be on track?
Then, based on that five-year goal, what is the ONE Thing I can do this year to be on track?
Then, based on my one-year goal, what is the ONE Thing I can do this month?
Based on my monthly goal, what is the ONE Thing I can do this week?
Based on my weekly goal, what is the ONE Thing I can do today?
And finally, based on my goal for today, what is the ONE Thing I can do right now?
Do you feel what just happened there?
You took a dream that lives way out on the horizon, and you built a chain of dominoes that runs all the way back to this very moment, to something you can do in the next hour.
That is the point.
A goal that is not connected to a present action is just a wish.
It floats out there and never touches your life.
But when you connect someday to five years, to one year, to this month, to this week, to today, to right now, suddenly your dream has a doorway, and the doorway is the next small thing you can do.
Goal Setting to the Now turns a distant, intimidating vision into a clear, doable next step.
And every time you take that next step, you knock over today's domino, which is lined up to knock over this week's, which is lined up for this month's, and so on up the chain to your someday.
Your big life is built out of the small right-now.
So let me pull all of this together, because underneath every technique in this book, there is a deeper question, and Keller does not shy away from it.
Why does any of this matter?
What is it all for?
The answer is purpose.
A big why.
The most powerful thing that can pull you through the hard mornings and the boring middles and the moments you want to quit is a reason that matters to you.
Purpose is what gives your ONE Thing its meaning.
It is the answer to the question, why is this the thing I am pouring my one life into?
When your ONE Thing is connected to a purpose you genuinely care about, the discipline becomes easier, the sacrifices feel worth it, and the whole machine runs on something deeper than willpower.
Purpose feeds priority, and priority drives productivity.
That is the chain.
Find your why, let it set your ONE priority, and let that priority determine what you produce.
Keller invites you to think about living a life worth living, and he reaches for one of the most famous stories in all of literature to make the point.
He asks you to remember Ebenezer Scrooge, from A Christmas Carol.
Scrooge spends his whole life chasing money and ignoring everything that actually matters, the people, the connection, the generosity, the love.
And then the Ghost of Christmas Yet to Come shows him the end.
It shows him his own grave, and a world that is not better for his having lived in it, a death mourned by no one.
Scrooge is horrified.
And in that horror, he is given a gift, the chance to wake up and change while there is still time.
That is the invitation Keller is extending to you, minus the ghosts.
Live with the end in mind.
Imagine yourself at the end of your life, looking back.
What will you wish you had focused on?
What ONE Thing, pursued with everything you had, would have made your life extraordinary and meaningful?
Because here is the sobering truth.
Your days are finite.
The dominoes you line up today are the life you will have built when you get to the end.
If you spend your one life scattered across a thousand things that do not matter, you will arrive at the end with a to-do list of regrets.

But if you find your ONE Thing, the thing connected to your deepest purpose, and you commit to it, you can build something that ripples out far beyond you, a chain of falling dominoes that reaches, in its own way, toward the moon.
Let me leave you with something concrete, because ideas are only worth as much as the action they produce.
So here is what I want you to actually do this week.
First, write down the Focusing Question and put it somewhere you will see it every day.
What is the ONE Thing I can do such that by doing it everything else will be easier or unnecessary?
Tape it to your monitor.
Put it on your phone's lock screen.
Let it become the question you ask yourself before you dive into your day.
Second, tomorrow morning, before you touch your email or your phone or the news, ask that question about your work.
Out of everything on your plate, what is the single most important thing, the domino that makes the rest easier or unnecessary?
Then do that thing first, while your willpower battery is fully charged.
Just try it for one day and notice how different the day feels.
Third, open your calendar right now, before you forget, and block time for your ONE Thing.
Put in a real block, in the morning if you can, and protect it like a mother bear protects her cubs.
When something tries to steal that time this week, practice saying no, graciously but firmly.
Remember, every no to a distraction is a yes to what matters.
Fourth, pick one habit, just one, that would move your life forward, and commit to it for the next sixty-six days.
Not ten habits.
One.
Pour your discipline into that single habit until it becomes automatic, and let everything else wait its turn.
Fifth, do the Goal Setting to the Now exercise on paper.
Start with your someday goal and walk it back.
Five years.
One year.
This month.
This week.
Today.
Right now.
Find the one small thing you can do in the next hour that is connected all the way up to your biggest dream, and then go do it.
And finally, this week, sit with the big why.
Ask yourself what your ONE Thing is really for, what purpose it serves, what you would want to see when you look back at the end.
Let that answer choose your priority, and let that priority shape your days.
That is The ONE Thing by Gary Keller and Jay Papasan.
Extraordinary results come from going small, not doing more.
From lining up your dominoes and knocking over the right one first.

From protecting your focus against the six lies and the four thieves, from counterbalancing instead of chasing perfect balance, from spending your willpower early on what matters most, and from connecting the small action in front of you to the big life you want to build.
You do not need to do everything.
You need to do the ONE Thing.
Find it, protect it, and let it topple the rest.
Now go knock over your first domino.
I am rooting for you.
